title: Twined weaving on a frame loom
subtitle: Using two weft threads that twist around every warp end to produce a firm, locked cloth
excerpt: Twined weaving uses two weft threads worked simultaneously. Instead of passing one thread over and under alternate warps, the two threads cross each other between every warp end, locking the structure. The resulting cloth is stiffer and more dimensionally stable than plain weave, and is suited to bags, mats, and small rugs worked on a simple frame loom.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from Native North American and West African textile tradition. Twining is documented in 19th-century ethnographic textile records (public domain). Structural description draws on Emery, The Primary Structures of Fabrics (1966), cited for technical substance only; written around in plain prose. No quoted text.

glossaryTerms.definition: A weft-interlacing structure in which two or more weft elements are twisted around each other between each warp end, rather than passing alternately over and under adjacent warps. The twist locks every warp end in place, producing a cloth that resists distortion without a beater. Twining predates the heddle loom and appears across textile traditions worldwide.

The half-turn of one weft thread over the other that occurs between each warp end in twined weaving. An S-twist (left-over-right) and a Z-twist (right-over-left) create different surface textures when worked consistently or alternated. Consistent twist direction produces a smooth diagonal rib; alternating twist direction produces a horizontal twill-like line.

content.text: Twined weaving on a frame loom uses two weft threads simultaneously. Each pair of weft threads crosses over each other between every warp end: this is the

action. The crossed threads lock around every warp end and hold it in place without the use of a heddle or a beater. The cloth that results is considerably stiffer than plain weave and does not distort under lateral stress. This makes twining well suited to bags, flat mats, and small structural panels that need to hold a shape. A simple frame loom is all that is required: no shed mechanism, no shuttle, just two butterflies of weft yarn and steady hands.

items.name: Frame loom, any size suitable for the project
Warp yarn: smooth, firm cotton or linen, 8/2 or 10/2 weight
items.qty: Enough for the planned warp at 8-10 ends per cm

items.name: Weft yarn: wool, cotton, or linen in two colours or one
items.qty: Two butterflies, each 3-4 m per 10 cm of width

items.name: Blunt darning needle (for finishing warp and weft tails)

content.text: Warp the frame at 8-10 warp ends per 10 cm. A firm, smooth warp yarn resists the lateral twisting force of the weft twists better than a soft woollen warp. Tie on at the bottom left peg or notch. Wind straight across the frame to the top, then return to the bottom in a figure-of-eight or straight warp according to the frame design. Keep even tension across all ends and tie off securely at the bottom right. The warp should feel firm but not overly taut: a firmly pushed warp end should spring back to centre.

Wind two butterflies of weft yarn, each holding 3-4 m per 10 cm of weaving width. Both butterflies begin together at the left selvedge. Bring both threads to the front, between the first and second warp end. You are now ready to begin twining.
Step 3: Work the first twined row

Take the lower thread and pass it in front of the first warp end, then to the back between the first and second warp ends. Now take the upper thread (which was behind) and bring it forward between the second and third warp ends, passing it over the first thread as it crosses. This half-turn is the

. Continue across the row, alternating which thread passes in front, with one twist between every warp end. At the right selvedge, loop both threads around the edge warp end and return left. To produce an even surface, keep the twist direction consistent throughout each row. An S-twist (the thread coming from the left goes over the one from the right) gives a faint left-leaning rib; a Z-twist gives a right-leaning rib. Alternating S and Z twist row by row produces a horizontal line pattern.
Step 4: Beat each row and advance

After completing each row, press the weft down with the fingers or the weaving comb. Twined cloth does not require a heavy beat because the crossing threads hold their position. Three or four firm passes with the comb are enough. Check the selvedges: they should neither pull in nor loop out. A consistent, light tension on each butterfly as it crosses keeps the edges true.
Step 5: Finish and remove from the frame

At the end of the weaving, work two rows of plain twining close together to create a firm selvedge edge. Remove the warp loops from the frame notches carefully, keeping them as loops where possible. Thread a blunt darning needle with each warp tail and weave back through two or three rows of the twining before trimming. Weft tails can be threaded through the twined rows in the same way. Do not pull the tails tight or the edge rows will distort.

items.problem: The selvedges draw in progressively as the piece grows
items.cause: The weft butterflies are pulled too tight when twisting, drawing the edge warps towards the centre
items.fix: Leave a slight arc of slack in the weft thread before pressing it down with the comb. The twisting action will pull the arc flat; if the weft starts straight, it will pull the edge in.

items.problem: The twist direction reverses mid-row, producing an uneven surface
items.cause: The two butterflies have been picked up in the wrong order at some point, swapping which one leads
items.fix: Mark one butterfly with a small knot or colour tie. It should always be the thread that comes forward from the back of the loom. If you find you have picked up the unmarked thread, simply swap them back to the correct positions before continuing the row.

items.problem: The warp ends bunch together in some areas and spread apart in others
items.cause: The frame warp was wound with uneven tension, or the beating pressure varied across the width
items.fix: Re-warp the frame with steady tension, tightening each end to the same firmness before tying off. Use the weaving comb to spread and even the warp ends at the start of the first few rows.
